mechanism: An experimental design comparing three policy regimes (quota, preferential treatment, control) in a competitive task environment. Participants perform a standardized task; outcomes are measured as task success rate and composition of winning candidates. The method assesses whether policy-induced changes to the candidate pool composition affect the aggregate performance metric, using group-level comparison of means across treatment conditions.

Measurement of individual differences in cognitive processing style (analyticity) via established psychometric instruments. Correlation analysis between analyticity and religious belief scores. Four experimental manipulations (priming tasks) induce analytic processing; outcomes (religious belief) measured pre- and post-manipulation via self-report. Statistical inference compares belief outcomes across manipulation conditions relative to control.

Participants perform sequences of episodic memory tasks (encoding novel objects, retrieving old objects). Subsequent memory performance on discrimination tasks is measured and compared based on the type of prior memory operation. Analysis assesses how recent encoding and retrieval operations bias subsequent memory processing via difference scores or effect size estimation across task conditions.

Experimental design manipulating resource scarcity (cognitive load, financial constraint scenarios). Outcome measurement of attention allocation (task focus, distraction) and behavioral choice (borrowing, spending decisions) via multiple dependent variables. Group comparison of attention and choice metrics across scarcity and control conditions.

Participants view videos of human actors displaying emotions under different conditions (faces alone, bodies alone, combined). Emotional valence (positive/negative) is classified via binary judgment or dimensional rating. Accuracy and error patterns are compared across viewing conditions. Compound stimuli (mismatched face/body emotions) test attribution mechanisms via shift in classification outcomes.

Two quasi-experimental designs: (1) induced financial priming (thought generation) before cognitive test administration; (2) repeated cognitive measurement on the same individuals at two time points (poor harvest season vs. rich post-harvest). Cognitive performance scores are compared pre-post within individuals and between primed/control groups. Mediation analysis examines whether stress, time, nutrition account for performance differences.

Participants randomly assigned to read different text types (literary fiction, popular fiction, nonfiction, or control). Post-reading assessment of theory-of-mind (ToM) ability via standardized psychological tests measuring affective and cognitive mentalizing. Group comparison of test scores across literary fiction and control/alternative conditions.

Participants are placed in a quiet room without external stimuli for a fixed duration. Self-reported pleasure/displeasure is measured via rating scales. Some participants offered a choice to self-administer mild aversive stimulus (electric shock). Outcome metric is duration engaged with stimulus and preference for stimulus over solitude.

Laboratory experiment: participants make binary donation decisions under conditions where overhead costs are either borne by the donor or covered by seed funding. Outcome is binary donation choice. Field experiment (n=40,000): donors receive information about overhead coverage via initial funding announcement; outcome is donation rate and total amount. Comparison of donation rates across three information conditions (overhead-free, matching, seed funding).

Seven-month-old infants are exposed to an artificial language with two grammatical structures repeated in sequence (e.g., ABB vs. AAB patterns). Infants' looking time (visual attention duration) is measured via eye-gaze recording. Test stimuli with novel vocabulary but same or different grammatical structure are presented; looking time to novel vs. familiar structures is compared.

Participants evaluate decision problems presented in logically equivalent but semantically different frames (e.g., gains vs. losses). Preference choices are recorded and compared across frames. Reversals in preference are identified when the same decision option is chosen with different frequencies depending on frame. Analysis documents frequency of preference reversal across multiple problem types.

Static analysis tool is applied to a large corpus of student Java programs (10 million errors across 500k submissions). Error types are categorized and counted. Frequencies of error types are compared across student cohorts (majors vs. non-majors) and tracked over time (progression through course sequence). Correlation analysis assesses association between error frequencies and eventual program correctness.

Participants (online raters) evaluate hypothetical Airbnb host profiles. Profiles are labeled or suspected to be AI-written or human-written. Trustworthiness rating is measured via Likert scale. Comparison of ratings across AI/human/mixed conditions identifies trust penalty associated with AI authorship. Interaction analysis examines whether effect depends on profile composition (pure AI vs. mixed AI+human).

Randomized crossover design: subjects undergo cognitive fatigue induction (demanding task) or control (passive viewing). Time to physical exhaustion (cycling to 80% peak power) is measured as primary outcome. Secondary measures include perceived exertion, mood, and physiological variables (heart rate, lactate). Comparison of exhaustion times between fatigued and control conditions; mediation analysis examines physiological vs. perceptual mechanisms.

Survey respondents are presented with scenarios describing monetary and inflation changes. Responses (behavioral choices, willingness-to-pay, or agreement ratings) are analyzed to identify systematic deviations from real (inflation-adjusted) value reasoning. Comparison of response patterns to nominal vs. real framings reveals bias toward nominal representation. Measurement of effect frequency across diverse scenarios.

Individual differences in a cognitive control task (using task context to override habitual responses) are measured via established paradigm. Separately, model-based vs. model-free choice behavior is measured in a sequential decision task (two-stage task). Correlation analysis assesses whether cognitive control performance predicts the proportion of choices attributable to model-based vs. model-free strategy.

Participants solve a task under two compensation schemes: piece-rate (non-competitive) and tournament (competitive). Performance is measured under both schemes. Participants then choose which scheme to use for a subsequent task; binary choice (tournament vs. piece-rate) is recorded. Comparison of tournament selection rates by gender; regression analysis assesses whether performance, overconfidence, or risk preference mediate the gender gap.

Participants evaluate vignettes describing an agent's actions and psychological states. Judgments are recorded on two dimensions: whether the action was performed intentionally and whether the agent had an intention. Responses are categorized and compared across scenarios with systematically varied moral valences and psychological descriptions. Analysis examines whether moral status (good vs. bad behavior) influences intentionality judgment differently from intent judgment.

Participants evaluate scenarios describing an agent's behavior under different causal framings (free will vs. determinism contexts). Judgment of agency and moral responsibility is recorded via Likert or categorical rating. Comparison of responsibility attributions across scenarios reveals context-dependent shifts between incompatibilist and compatibilist reasoning patterns.

Hiring managers evaluate job candidates and make binary accept/reject hiring decisions. Candidate profiles vary in capability level (high vs. low). Mediator measures (organizational interest, flight risk perception) and outcome (hiring decision) are collected. Analysis quantifies how capability perception affects hiring probability and tests mediation through organizational interest and outside job options.
